sound_type: warning foot-stamp / hiss -- a multi-modal defensive warning display combining physical percussion (rapid front-foot stamping), respiratory threat (sustained hissing), and visual aposematic display (tail raise, black-and-white coloring), the 70th acoustic element, the first multi-modal warning in the engine, NOT a traditional vocalization
ecosystem: woodland edge -- the transitional zone between forest and open land, brushy areas, agricultural margins, suburban neighborhoods, mixed habitats with ground-level cover and open foraging areas, same ecotone as the Short-tailed Weasel (degree 67)
season: year-round resident throughout Washington State, from lowlands to mid-elevation

coordinates.description: Reference for woodland edge habitat in the Puget Sound lowlands. Striped Skunks are widespread across Washington from lowlands to mid-elevation, inhabiting woodland edges, suburban margins, agricultural land, and mixed habitats. Warning displays most commonly observed during close encounters at dusk and dawn.

taxonomy_note.description: Tenth mammalian entry. Eighth mammalian species. Eighth Carnivora, first Mephitidae. NEW FAMILY (29th). THE MUSTELIDAE SEXTET BREAKS -- after six consecutive Mustelidae (63-68), degree 69 brings the sister family Mephitidae. Skunks WERE Mustelidae until molecular phylogenetics placed them in their own family. Mephitidae is Mustelidae's closest sister group. The break is real taxonomically but minimal phylogenetically.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Mephitis from Latin mephitis (noxious vapor, pestilential exhalation), referring to the defensive spray. In Roman mythology, Mefitis was the goddess of sulfurous volcanic fumes. The species epithet repeats the genus: Mephitis mephitis -- a tautonym, stating the defining characteristic twice. Common name 'Striped Skunk' refers to the white dorsal stripe. 'Skunk' derives from Algonquian seganku or squnck, meaning 'one who squirts.'
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Mephitis -- two species: M. mephitis (Striped Skunk) and M. macroura (Hooded Skunk). The Striped Skunk is the most widespread and common skunk in North America. The genus name references the noxious spray that defines the family.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Carnivora -- eighth entry. The FIRST non-Mustelidae Carnivora entry. Seven previous carnivores (Otter 63, Mink 64, Marten 65, Fisher 66, Short-tailed Weasel 67, Long-tailed Weasel 68) were all Mustelidae. The skunk breaks the single-family dominance. Eight carnivores, two families, one superfamily (Musteloidea).
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- tenth mammalian entry. Eighth mammalian species. Mammalian representation rises to 14.3% (10 of 70 entries). The mammalian era (degrees 61-69) is now nine degrees deep. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), four families (Castoridae, Mustelidae, Mephitidae).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Mephitidae's FIRST entry at degree 69. The 29th family in the engine. NEW FAMILY. Mephitidae is Mustelidae's closest sister group -- skunks were classified in Mustelidae until molecular phylogenetic analysis (Dragoo & Honeycutt 1997, Flynn et al. 2005) revealed a deep divergence (~30-40 million years). The reclassification is universally accepted. The family contains 4 genera and 12 species worldwide.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Variable and multi-component. Foot-stamp: broadband percussive impact, no tonal center. Hiss: low-frequency broadband noise, approximately 500-4,000 Hz, centered in the 1,000-2,500 Hz range. Not a tonal vocalization.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Foot-stamp: broadband impact percussion. Hiss: 1,000-2,500 Hz (the breathy, forced-exhalation band)
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song or vocalization. A WARNING DISPLAY. Multi-modal: (1) Foot-stamping -- rapid percussive impacts of front feet against substrate. (2) Hissing -- sustained forceful exhalation through teeth and nostrils. (3) Tail raising -- visual aposematic display. Additional vocalizations: growling (close-range), squealing (distress, kit contact). The foot-stamp / hiss dominates -- the sound of escalating chemical threat.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Foot-stamp sequences: 1-5 seconds of rapid stamping. Hissing: 1-3 seconds per hiss, may repeat. Full warning display (from initial posturing through spray or retreat): 5-30 seconds. The display is a sequence, not a single event.
song_metrics.note_structure: MULTI-MODAL WARNING SEQUENCE: (1) Foot-stamp: rapid percussive impacts, sharp transient sounds without tonal quality. (2) Hiss: sustained broadband noise, forceful exhalation. (3) Visual: tail raise, body arch, aposematic pattern exposure. The sequence is an escalation ladder, not a single note structure.
song_metrics.note_types: Foot-stamp (percussive, rapid, sharp impacts), hiss (sustained, breathy, low-pitched broadband), growl (close-range threat, rare), squeal (distress/contact, high-pitched). The foot-stamp / hiss combination is the Striped Skunk's signature -- the acoustic preamble to chemical defense.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Escalation ladder: posturing -> foot-stamping -> hissing -> tail raising -> body arching -> spray. Most encounters end at the foot-stamp or hiss stage. The acoustic elements (stamp, hiss) are embedded in a multi-channel behavioral sequence. The phrase IS the escalation.
song_metrics.timbre: Foot-stamp: sharp, percussive, transient -- like rapid hand-clapping against hard ground. Hiss: breathy, forceful, sustained -- more similar to a cat's hiss than to any mustelid vocalization. Combined: percussive sharpness followed by sustained threat. Defensive rather than aggressive in character.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Common but not abundant. The warning display is moderately loud -- audible at close range (5-15 meters) but not a long-distance signal. The hiss is quieter than the weasels' chattering (E=4) but more sustained. The real 'amplitude' of the skunk's defense is chemical, not acoustic.
song_metrics.tempo: Foot-stamp: rapid (4-8 stamps per second during peak agitation). Hiss: sustained (1-3 seconds). The tempo accelerates as the threat escalates -- faster stamping, more frequent hissing, leading toward spray deployment.
song_metrics.context: DEFENSIVE WARNING. Produced when the skunk is threatened, cornered, or approached too closely. The warning display is NOT aggressive -- it is a request to be left alone. The skunk deploys the display as an alternative to spraying, which is a finite resource. Most encounters produce the foot-stamp / hiss; few produce the spray.
song_metrics.regional_variation: NO documented regional variation in warning display behavior. The foot-stamp / hiss / spray sequence is consistent across the species' range.
song_metrics.key_property: THE WARNING FOOT-STAMP / HISS -- the 70th acoustic element. The first MULTI-MODAL WARNING DISPLAY in the engine. Physical percussion (foot-stamp) + respiratory threat (hiss) + visual aposematic display (tail raise + black-and-white coloring). E=3. Defensive, not aggressive. The sound of escalation toward chemical defense. NOT a traditional vocalization.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized, stocky musteloid. 520-770 mm total length, 1.8-4.5 kg. Substantially larger than both weasels (150-450 g). Plantigrade stance (walks on soles of feet). Short legs, broad body -- the opposite of the elongated weasel bauplan. Built for slow, deliberate movement rather than burrow pursuit. The chemical defense makes speed unnecessary.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 69, the diagnostic features are: (1) APOSEMATIC COLORING -- black with prominent white dorsal stripe splitting into a V at the nape and running along both flanks. (2) WHITE FACIAL STRIPE from forehead to nose. (3) BUSHY TAIL -- black with white intermixed, raised during warning display. (4) STOCKY BUILD -- low-slung, broad, plantigrade. (5) LONG CURVED FORECLAWS -- adapted for digging (insects, grubs). (6) SLOW, DELIBERATE GAIT -- waddle.
physical_description.fur_detail: BASE: Deep, consistent black across body, legs, and face. DORSAL STRIPE: Prominent white stripe beginning as a thin line on the forehead, widening across the crown, splitting into a V at the nape, and running along both sides of the back to the tail. Stripe pattern varies individually -- no two Striped Skunks have identical patterns. FACIAL STRIPE: Thin white line from forehead to nose tip. TAIL: Large, bushy, black with variable white. FUNCTION: Aposematic warning coloration -- high-contrast black-and-white visible at night, evolved to be remembered after one encounter.
physical_description.dental_detail: 34 teeth. Less specialized carnassials than the mustelids -- reflecting the omnivorous diet. The dental formula accommodates crushing (insects, grubs) as well as cutting (small vertebrates). Less specialized than the weasels' hypercarnivore dentition.
physical_description.field_id: At woodland edge or suburban margins: a medium-sized, low-slung, stocky black mammal with a prominent white dorsal stripe pattern and bushy tail. Moves with a slow, deliberate waddle. Primarily nocturnal/crepuscular. Often detected by smell before sight. When alarmed, stamps front feet, hisses, and raises tail. Distinguished from all other PNW mammals by the black-and-white stripe pattern and the unmistakable odor.

nodes.title: The Mustelidae Sextet Breaks -- Mephitidae (29th Family)
nodes.description: After six consecutive Mustelidae (63-68), the streak breaks with the sister family Mephitidae at degree 69. The 29th family. NEW FAMILY. Skunks WERE Mustelidae until molecular phylogenetics reclassified them. Mephitidae is Mustelidae's closest sister group. The break is real but paradoxical -- the closest possible non-Mustelidae family. Seven consecutive musteloid degrees (63-69) at the superfamily level.

nodes.title: The Warning Foot-Stamp / Hiss -- 70th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Multi-modal defensive warning: foot-stamping (physical percussion) + hissing (respiratory threat) + tail raise (visual aposematic display). E=3. The FIRST multi-modal warning display in the engine. NOT a traditional vocalization. The most complex warning in 70 degrees. Defensive, not aggressive -- the sound of escalation toward chemical defense.

nodes.title: Chemical Defense -- Only Chemical Weapon in the Engine
nodes.description: N-butyl mercaptan (butanethiol) spray from paired anal glands. Directed at targets 3-5 meters. Detectable 1.5 km downwind. The ONLY species in 70 entries with a chemical weapon. A Mephitidae innovation -- the weaponization of anal scent glands that all musteloids possess. The spray is a last resort; the foot-stamp / hiss warning is the escalation ladder.

nodes.title: The Skunk Paradox -- Former Mustelidae, Now Sister Family
nodes.description: Skunks were classified in Mustelidae for over a century. Molecular phylogenetics (Dragoo & Honeycutt 1997, Flynn et al. 2005) reclassified them into Mephitidae. If the reclassification had not occurred, degree 69 would extend the Mustelidae streak to seven consecutive. The 'break' depends on a 1997 paper. The boundary is real, recent, and paper-thin.

nodes.title: Woodland Edge -- Shared Ecotone with Short-tailed Weasel
nodes.description: Both the Striped Skunk (69) and the Short-tailed Weasel (67) inhabit woodland edge. Same ecotone, opposite strategies: weasel = specialist predator, speed, aggression. Skunk = omnivorous forager, slow movement, chemical defense. Second edge-habitat portfolio species. The edge supports both extremes.

nodes.title: Eighth Carnivore -- First Non-Mustelidae
nodes.description: Carnivora grows to eight entries. The Striped Skunk is the FIRST non-Mustelidae Carnivora entry. Seven previous carnivores (63-68) were all Mustelidae. Carnivora's representation now spans two families (Mustelidae, Mephitidae), both within the superfamily Musteloidea. No non-musteloid carnivore yet.

nodes.title: First Omnivore in the Carnivora Sequence
nodes.description: After seven hypercarnivorous mustelids (Otter through Long-tailed Weasel), the eighth Carnivora entry is an omnivore. The Striped Skunk eats insects, grubs, fruit, small mammals, eggs, and carrion. Classified in Carnivora by ancestry, not by current dietary specialization. The order name describes evolutionary history, not ecological role.

nodes.title: Aposematic Coloring -- Warning Display
nodes.description: High-contrast black-and-white dorsal stripe pattern. Aposematic: warning coloration advertising the chemical defense. Visible at night (nocturnal species). Evolved to be remembered after a single encounter. The visual component of the three-channel warning system (visual + acoustic + chemical). First aposematic species in the engine.

nodes.title: The Tautonym -- Mephitis mephitis
nodes.description: Mephitis mephitis -- the genus and species are the same word. 'Noxious vapor noxious vapor.' A tautonym emphasizing the defining characteristic so emphatically that taxonomy states it twice. The first and only tautonym in the engine.

nodes.title: Ninth Consecutive Original CSV Species
nodes.description: Degrees 61-69: nine consecutive original CSV species. The longest original-CSV streak in engine history extends yet again. Every mammalian entry has been an original CSV species. No substitutions needed or considered.

nodes.title: Tenth Mammalian Entry -- The Mammalian Era Extends
nodes.description: Ten mammalian entries across nine degrees (61-69). Eight mammalian species. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), four families (Castoridae, Mustelidae, Mephitidae). Mammalian representation rises to 14.3% (10 of 70 entries). The mammalian era is nine degrees deep. Critical imbalance.

nodes.title: The Kris Orlowski Parallel
nodes.description: Both unassuming until provoked. Orlowski: quiet Seattle singer-songwriter, E=2, community musician. Striped Skunk: slow-walking chemical defender, E=3. Both carry quiet authority. Neither looks for trouble. Both will stand their ground absolutely. Less dramatic energy contrast than degree 68 but more thematically unified.

cross_references.relationship: BOTH WOODLAND-EDGE SPECIES. Same ecotone habitat. Predator vs. chemical defender. Speed vs. deterrence. The weasel pursues prey into burrows; the skunk walks slowly because nothing chases it. Same habitat, opposite survival strategies.

THE MUSTELIDAE SEXTET ENDS, THE SISTER FAMILY ARRIVES. The last Mustelidae (68) followed by the first Mephitidae (69). The closest possible taxonomic transition without staying in Mustelidae. Open woodland (68) to woodland edge (69) -- the habitat gradient continues.

MUSTELOIDEA BOOKENDS. Mustelidae opened at 63, sister family Mephitidae at 69. Seven consecutive musteloid degrees. The superfamily Musteloidea spans water (Otter, river/estuary) to woodland edge (Skunk). Social chuff (63) to defensive foot-stamp/hiss (69).
BOTH UBIQUITOUS. Both universally recognized PNW species. Everyone has encountered both -- the robin by sight and song, the skunk by smell. Two of the most familiar wild species in the region.
BOTH 'THE DEFAULT.' Mallard = default duck. Striped Skunk = default nocturnal mammal. Both are the first species that comes to mind when their category is mentioned. Both culturally omnipresent.
Tenth mammalian entry. Eighth mammalian species. Eighth Carnivora, first Mephitidae. Mammalian representation rises to 14.3% (10 of 70 entries). Two orders, four families, eight species.

Woodland edge habitat. Chemical defense as ecological strategy. Omnivorous diet (insects, grubs, fruit, small vertebrates). Second edge specialist after the Short-tailed Weasel. Aposematic coloration. Multi-modal warning display.
